fuzzy_prd: Our API endpoints need to serialize domain objects to JSON in a controlled way. Right now every endpoint hand-crafts its own JSON, leading to inconsistent field selection, key naming, and nesting. We need a declarative serialization engine where a developer describes what the output should look like and the engine does the transformation. The spec should support: choosing exactly which fields appear and in what order; optionally wrapping the output under a root key (with separate keys for single vs collection); rendering a collection as either a plain array or as a lookup object keyed by a chosen field; embedding related records and inline groupings at arbitrary nesting levels with optional output key renaming; transforming key casing to match the naming convention of the API consumer (camel, lower-camel, dash, snake); conditionally including a field based on a predicate checked against the record value or request parameters; substituting a fixed placeholder when a field value is null; silently dropping or nullifying fields that fail to resolve; and attaching metadata like a count. Runtime overrides (a root key supplied at request time) should take precedence over the spec declaration. Key transformation must be optionally cascaded into nested associations — look at how the existing 'strict' transformation mode is wired in the transformation pipeline to understand whether cascade is the default.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature:
Alba.serialize(object, resource:, root_key: nil, params: {}, meta: {})
class MyResource
  include Alba::Resource
  attributes :field1, :field2
  association :relation, resource: OtherResource
  transform_keys :camel
  root_key :singular, :plural
end

trigger_keywords: is key transformation cascaded by default
does nested association inherit key transformation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Key transformation cascade defaults to false. When cascade is not explicitly set to true, a parent's key transformation does NOT propagate to nested associations; the nested association keeps its original field names.

trigger_keywords: does runtime root_key override resource declaration
request-time root key vs declared root key
root_key supplied in request takes precedence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes. A root_key supplied at serialization call time overrides any root_key declared in the resource specification. The runtime key wraps both singular and collection payloads.
When on_nil is set to a constant value, every attribute whose resolved value is null is replaced by that constant in the output. The constant is emitted as-is in the JSON.

trigger_keywords: difference between ignore and nullify error policy
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: 'ignore': an attribute that raises an error is silently dropped from the output (not emitted at all). 'nullify': an attribute that raises an error is included in the output with a JSON null value.

trigger_keywords: difference between camel and lower_camel styles
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: 'camel' produces UpperCamelCase (every word capitalized, including the first). 'lower_camel' produces lowerCamelCase (first word lowercase, subsequent words capitalized). 'dash' produces kebab-case. 'snake' preserves underscore_case.

trigger_keywords: meta count_key adds what to output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When meta declares a count_key, the number of serialized records (collection size) is added to the output under the given key. For a single object, count is 1. The meta object is merged into the top-level output alongside the data.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: how the existing 'strict' transformation mode is wired in the transformation pipeline
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The transform_keys option in Alba::Resource. The 'cascade' sub-option controls whether the transformation propagates into nested associations. By default cascade is false, meaning each association is independent.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Refer to the lib/ directory for the Alba::Resource serialization engine and rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the complete contract for each feature.

feature1_attribute_selection.json.description: Serialize a record into a JSON object that contains only an explicitly selected set of fields, in the exact order they were declared. A single record produces a JSON object; a list of records produces a JSON array of objects. Fields present on the record but not selected are omitted.

feature2_1_explicit_root_key.json.description: Wrap the serialized payload under a top-level root key. The resource declares a singular root key and a separate plural root key for collections; a single record is wrapped under the singular key while a list of records is wrapped under the collection key.

feature2_2_runtime_root_key.json.description: A root key supplied at serialization time overrides any root key declared on the resource. The payload is wrapped under the request-supplied key regardless of the resource's own root-key configuration.

feature2_3_collection_root_key.json.description: A resource may declare a root key that applies only when serializing a collection. When given a list of records, the output is wrapped under that collection key; each record contributes one object to the wrapped array.

feature3_1_collection_array.json.description: When a list of records is serialized without any root key, the result is a bare JSON array of objects, one per record, preserving input order.

feature3_2_collection_key.json.description: A collection can be serialized as a JSON object keyed by a chosen field of each record instead of as an array. Each record becomes a value under the string form of that record's key field.

feature4_1_one_association.json.description: A singular association embeds a related record as a nested JSON object under the association name. The nested object is itself serialized with its own selected fields.

feature4_2_many_association.json.description: A plural association embeds a list of related records as a nested JSON array under the association name. Each element is serialized with the association resource's selected fields, preserving order.

feature4_3_association_key.json.description: An association can be rendered under an explicit output key that differs from the association name. The nested payload appears under the chosen key instead of the source field name.

feature4_4_nil_association.json.description: When the source field backing an association is absent (null), the association serializes to JSON null, for both singular and plural associations.

feature4_5_nested_attribute.json.description: A nested attribute group introduces an arbitrary nesting level in the output that is not backed by an association on the source record. Fields listed for the group are read from the same source record and placed under the group's key as a nested object.

feature5_1_key_transform.json.description: Output keys can be rewritten according to a casing style applied uniformly to every attribute key. Supported styles are camel (UpperCamelCase), lower_camel (lowerCamelCase), dash (kebab-case), snake (snake_case) and none (unchanged). Underscore-delimited source field names are the input to the transformation.

feature5_2_root_key_transform.json.description: Key transformation can optionally be applied to the root key as well. When root transformation is enabled, the wrapping root key is transformed with the same style as the inner keys; when it is disabled, the root key is left untouched while inner keys are still transformed.

feature5_3_cascade.json.description: Key transformation can cascade into inline associations or be confined to the top level. With cascade enabled, nested association keys are transformed with the same style as the parent; with cascade disabled, the parent's keys are transformed while the nested association keeps its original field names.

feature6_1_conditional_attribute_value.j.description: An attribute can be made conditional on a predicate evaluated against the attribute's own value. The attribute is included only when the predicate holds (here: the value's length is at least a threshold) and is otherwise omitted entirely from the output.

feature6_2_conditional_attribute_param.j.description: An attribute can be made conditional on a request-supplied parameter rather than on record data. The attribute is included only when the named parameter is truthy; when the parameter is falsey or absent, the attribute is omitted.

feature6_3_conditional_one.json.description: A singular association can be made conditional on a predicate evaluated against the associated record. The association is embedded only when the predicate holds (here: a field of the associated record ends with a given suffix); otherwise the association key is omitted entirely.

feature6_4_conditional_many.json.description: A plural association can be made conditional on a predicate evaluated against the associated collection. The association is embedded only when the predicate holds (here: the collection is non-empty); when it does not hold, the association key is omitted entirely.

feature7_1_on_nil.json.description: A nil-handling policy can replace every null attribute value with a configured constant. Attributes whose source value is null are emitted with the replacement constant instead of null; non-null values pass through unchanged.

feature7_2_on_error.json.description: When an attribute cannot be resolved (the source record does not provide it), an error-handling policy decides the outcome. The 'ignore' policy drops the offending attribute from the output; the 'nullify' policy emits it as null; the default policy propagates the failure, which the adapter reports as a neutral serialization error.

feature8_meta.json.description: A resource may declare metadata that is attached under a top-level 'meta' key, but only when the payload itself is wrapped under a root key. The metadata may be a static map for a single record and a derived value (such as the collection size) for a list. Metadata supplied at serialization time is merged into the declared metadata, overriding entries on key collisions.

feature9_invalid_transform.json.description: Requesting an unsupported key-transformation style is rejected when the resource definition is built. The adapter reports a neutral error identifying the failure category and echoing the offending style value, rather than producing any serialized output.
